data.title: Strategy: Bitcoin As A Treasury Model Faces Stress Test
data.description:
Strategy's (MSTR) Bitcoin-as-a-Treasury ("BaaT") business model has dramatically imploded on the back of a 58% decline over the last 1-year. This has broadly eliminated an inherent tentpole premium that has formed a currency for MSTR's rapid accumulation of BTC since a sleepy enterprise software company operating out of Tyson's Corner, Virginia, decided to buy and hold BTC on its balance sheet in the summer of 2020. This new zeitgeist represents MSTR's most intense stress test. It could really come to represent a permanent step change in the company's prospects, a rollback of the highly lucrative BTC accumulation strategy that allowed the constant issuance of new common equity to buy BTC. This strategy has been ruthlessly effective, allowing MSTR to increase its BTC holdings to 712,647 as of its most recent dashboard update.

This is valued at $63.64 billion with BTC currently trading for roughly $89,294. Critically, with MSTR's enterprise value at $68.21 billion, the company's market-adjusted net asset value ("mNAV") has dipped to 1.07. This captures MSTR's enterprise value in relation to the value of its BTC holdings. A figure greater than 1x means the company can sell its common shares through its at-the-market offering program at a cadence that optimises its BTC yield. This yield is a measure of the percentage change in BTC owned per share in comparison to the ramp-up in shareholder dilution. MSTR's BTC yield since the start of 2026 is currently running at 0.4%, just 40 basis points above zero. This is markedly lower than a BTC yield through 2025 that came in at 22.8%. The company closed out 2024 with a BTC yield of 74.3%. I last covered MSTR with a hold rating.

Beyond A Future Crypto Winter And The Non-Cumulative Perpetual Stride Preferreds

MSTR's weighted average common shares outstanding have increased by 148.8% over the last three years, an annual pace of dilution of just under 50% per year. Critically, the company's strategy made sense when this pace of dilution was completed at a BTC yield of 74.3%. The impact of diminishing mNAV is one where MSTR's core investment proposition is fundamentally eroded. The worst-case scenario for the company centers on a possible prolonged BTC winter being paired with an mNAV that's below 1x. MSTR is planning for this scenario, and in December, updated the market that it had built a $2.2 billion cash buffer to support the continued payment of the coupon payments on its preferreds. MSTR faces annual dividend payments of at least $880 million on its universe of preferreds.

This sets up the company's 10.00% Series A Perpetual Stride Preferred Stock (STRD) as a possible investment at its current level. These are currently swapping hands for $72.83 per share, an 27% discount to their $100 per share liquidation value. However, this discount does not matter as much. They're perpetual and not callable. Hence, the probability of MSTR redeeming these at their liquidation value is near-zero. The uncertainty is whether the company can continue to meet the annual $10 per share coupon payments. The cash reserves mean MSTR has at least two and a half years of coupon payments, which places its 13.7% current dividend yield in focus. However, they're non-cumulative and were sold at $85 per share when they IPOed, a discount of 15% to their liquidation price. I considered these versus (STRK), which pays out an $8 per share annual coupon for a 9.4% current yield. They're also cumulative and trade higher at $85 per share due to this feature. However, I view the risk of a coupon suspension on the non-cumulative STRD as extremely low.

STRD is the only one of MSTR's outstanding preferreds that are non-cumulative, which does amp up their risk profile. This is compounded by the company's lack of cash flow, with MSTR's currency lying entirely with the value of its commons and preferreds. The company needs to be able to continue to issue these to maintain a going concern with its BaaT strategy. This strategy would fall apart if, after 2.5 years of a BTC winter, MSTR suspends STRD's coupon. I'd view such a move as triggering significant cross-selling, spiking yields across its capital stack, and tanking demand for its preferreds. This would limit the company's access to the depth of institutional capital needed to maintain BTC purchases.

Conclusion

MSTR's ongoing selloff is either a more permanent reorientation of capital away from the ticker or a transient disallocation that could represent a buying opportunity for BTC bulls. I keep my rating as a hold. Bears would be right to state that even if BTC does expand, the company's mNAV could still trade on the narrower premium it currently does versus its historical average. This would limit overall upside on the commons. STRD provides bulls a way to earn an income with limited risk of a suspension through to 2028. MSTR has also stated that it will liquidate its BTC holdings to maintain its coupon payments in the event its mNAV dips below 1x. This cash reserve, BTC holdings, and continued access to liquidity keep MSTR rated as a hold.

data.title: SBA Communications: Discounted Tower REIT With Upside Potential
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

SBA Communications (SBAC) has been quite volatile in recent years, with the macro impacts of the post-pandemic rate hikes taking their toll, while I believe the stock is now reflecting an attractive entry level that already takes into account several risks, offering a good price for a piece of this REIT that owns and operates wireless communications infrastructure globally.

SBA reported a solid Q3, beating the market’s expectations on both FFO per share and revenue, while the attributable AFFO reached $352.94 million, while also highlighting an industry-leading AFFO per share in Q3.

As for the guidance, SBA sees a slightly lower attributable AFFO of $1.366 billion to $1.390 billion in 2025, meaning between $12.69 to $12.91 per share. At midpoint, that’s about $1.378 billion in 2025, placing them at a P/AFFO ratio of roughly 14.1 today, which is not terrible.

Based on SBA’s latest report, we see an overall weak position, with current assets below their current liabilities and a very significant amount of leverage, although that’s not as uncommon for the industry. Meanwhile, the weighted average interest rate stood at 3.8%, while the weighted average maturity was approximately 3 years at the end of Q3.

However, note that the company is undergoing significant changes, with the Millicom (TIGO) asset acquisition and the sale of their Canadian assets after the quarter’s end.

The Millicom deal adds ~7,000 towers across 5 Central American countries (plus an agreement to build up to 2,500 additional sites), being valued at about $975 million and expanding their international presence, while the 500 owned and managed sites in Canada sold adds roughly $330 million, helping the company exit a “subscale market” while being expected to be immediately accretive to AFFO. After these, SBA should own over 46,000 tower sites worldwide, which is an increase of 40% since 2020.

SBA is expected to announce their Q4 Earnings on February 23, with Normalized FFO estimated at $3.80 per share and $725.8 million in revenue. On top of potential guidance or hints at their expectations for 2026 that could generate more volatility, I will pay close attention to their asset reshuffling following their lowered leverage target range to 6x to 7x net debt to adjusted EBITDA compared to 7x to 7.5x before.

Meanwhile, they should also offer more information regarding the churn related to the Sprint consolidation, which is expected to reach $51 million in 2025, which we should also keep in mind, while the company still noted uncertainty regarding DISH, which Crown Castle (CCI) recently cut ties with after a payment default, while American Tower (AMT) also filed a lawsuit against them.

Dividends & Buybacks

SBA pays a ~2.36% dividend yield, which is relatively small for a REIT, representing about $472.24 million per year (payout ratio of ~34.27% based on midpoint attributable AFFO), but they also have other ways of returning capital to shareholders and reinvesting, including another couple percentages worth of buybacks as well as significant acquisitions.

Risks & Opportunities

Although the company depends on relatively stable companies, it’s important to note that they carry significant amounts of debt, which limits their expansion capacity in high-rate environments like the current one.

Meanwhile, as a REIT with high leverage, they generally have limited access to capital, while the cost generally went up as a result of the environment, and even though they have a low dividend yield now (the cost of issuing), they don’t really issue stock, meaning they have to rely on relatively expensive debt or asset sales (as we’ve seen) to develop.

What’s worse is that the environment remains pressured, with the market having to take into account all the uncertainty around re-emerging tariffs, Fed’s independence worries once Powell’s term ends in May, more fear around the rising national debt, and now the rate check and JPY risks.

For reference, Japan is the largest foreign owner of US treasuries, and if they sell these in order to strengthen the Yen, it can pressure the USD and US treasuries at an already bad time, while a US intervention (which was ruled out) could’ve been just as worse, placing the market in a very complicated position. This can be only the beginning of a wider trend, while European funds were reportedly selling some of their treasuries as well.

The higher rate is ultimately a way for the market to demand a better reward given the new level of risk, while the rising national debt is also a point of concern, with recent warnings that it could soon grow faster than the US economy, which can trigger “six distinct types of fiscal crises.”

Still, SBA also talked about their revised financial policy and their new approach given the current environment, with the company’s CFO highlighting the following during their Q3 Earnings Call:

In summary, the investment-grade bond market is the deepest and most robust credit market, which we expect will provide us with many benefits. This includes reducing our overall cost of debt over time, lowering future refinancing risk and extending our weighted average maturity, all while maintaining our ability to pursue a robust share buyback program and be opportunistic on the M&A front.

However, especially in this environment, given the company’s very low average maturity, refinancing can turn out to be very expensive. As for the longer-term, especially if they manage to improve their debt schedule and potentially refinance at low rates while also continuing to expand their exposure to core assets, they can stand to benefit from several long-term tailwinds, with the company’s management highlighting the following:

So as you can see, there are a lot of positive things going on here at SBA. And the macro environment for mobile broadband growth is supportive of a bright future. Today, we are seeing a greater proliferation of 5G use cases, including fixed wireless access, which is nearing 15 million subscribers today with aspirations of over $20 million by 2028. Paired with increasing mobile data traffic, that's a heavy burden on today's networks. This will require ongoing network investment via overlays and densifications, including cell splitting, refarming of existing spectrum bands and newly acquired spectrum to meet those network needs.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, SBA’s high level of leverage combined with the low average maturity can expose them to more pressure if the environment remains like this or continues to weaken in the foreseeable future, which can put pressure not only on them as a company directly, but also on REITs and the industry in general.

Meanwhile, some of their clients may be stable and defensive (not all), but that doesn’t mean they are immune to economic weakness, which can lead to less opportunities for SBA to expand, with more pressure potentially leading to lower valuations.

Valuation

For SBA’s DCF to equity valuation, I’ll start with the company’s midpoint attributable AFFO expectation of $1.378 billion, followed by a CAGR of 4% to assume that they manage to improve following the recent transactions and revised financial policy, while the environment could also improve, followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a discount rate of 8.5%, with 107.56 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $25.55 billion, for an intrinsic value of $237.51 per share, which is above the current $187.77.

Conclusions

I rate SBAC a Buy, as even though the company can certainly see more pressure from the environment, the stock already reflects that, allowing for a solid margin of safety.

There are definitely risks to keep in mind - especially from the environment combined with their balance sheet - but they’ve been operating like this since forever, while the overall risk-reward seems to be in their favor.

data.title: Rates - Turning Japanese, I Really Think So
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

We don't often spotlight Asia here, but this month we're all about Japan. What's happening there has important echoes for core markets in the West. The dominant impulse is upward pressure on market rates (dramatic in Japan of late), but at the same time, an ongoing evolution toward normal rates (and curves). This remains a thing in both the eurozone and the US.

Pressure for higher rates in Japan is an important relative value driver

There has been quite a theme of consternation in Japan of late. It has crescendoed over the past week amid talk of imminent Bank of Japan intervention to secure a turnaround for the super-weak Japanese yen (JPY). The backstory here involves maintaining exceptionally low official rates.

Last week, the Bank of Japan (BoJ) left the official rate unchanged at 0.75%. In prior weeks, though, the 30yr JGB yield almost touched 4%. The markets have clearly decided that the BoJ is lagging in its rate-hike game, and the weakness of JPY tells a similar story (a recent note here shows how this presents an opportunity).

The wider core rates environment is sitting up and paying attention to all of this – and for good reason. First, what's happening in Japan represents a clear swing from the prior zero-rate environment to a more normal rates environment.

We noted almost a year ago that rate pressure to the upside was a thing. We also held out 2% as a viable neutral rate for the BoJ to aim for, eventually. The 10yr JGB yield shot above that level in the past few weeks, and remains on a medium-term journey higher. And why not? Japanese inflation is currently in the 2.5-3% territory.

Apart from the move away from deflation, there have been a few important landmarks along the way that point to upward pressure on Japanese rates. First, inflation has become a thing in most core markets, including the eurozone (the ECB is already at its neutral rate of 2%).

Second, the BoJ ended its policy of yield curve control in March 2024 (although the market reaction was initially muted by ongoing BoJ support buying). Third, wide rate differentials with other core centres were clearly proving detrimental to the JPY, suggesting the differentials were too high (Japanese rates too low).

And, while not front and center as an immediate issue, we can't ignore the inconvenient truth of Japan's mammoth debt/GDP ratio. While about half of Japanese Government Bonds (JGBs) are held by the official sector, and foreign ownership is low, there is still a massive debt obligation outstanding.

This means two key outcomes. First, the classic low-rate funding currency of recent decades may still be with us, but now at much higher and rising funding rates. Second, this impacts US and eurozone rates from a relative-value perspective, pressuring them higher from below.

Holders of JGBs and the build of debt - Latest data for September 2025

As normal as you like in the eurozone and the US

Our rates outlook for 2026 centres on a theme of chaos, but also one of normality for rates. The noise out of Japan pushes in the same direction, as we see the BoJ eventually getting to 2% (perhaps not in the current cycle). In the eurozone, the ECB is already at its neutral deposit rate of 2%. And the 10yr Euribor rate is close to the 3% area, with an almost 100bp curve.

In the US, the Federal Reserve has a 3% handle on its funds rate, and the market discount has it ending in the 3-3.5% range. Again, a picture of neutrality. The same holds for the 10yr Treasury yield at or about 4.2% – also not too deviant from neutral versus long-run averages.

Our twist on this is the identification of an upward bias to longer tenor rates, both in the US and eurozone (and Japan), with inflation and fiscal circumstances as drivers (explained more fully in the aforementioned rates outlook for 2026).

data.title: BNY Mellon Core Plus Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The Federal Open Market Committee cut the federal funds rate by 50 basis points (BP) in the fourth quarter of 2025 (with a cut each in October and December), taking it to a range of 3.50% to 3.75%. In December, three members dissented (the highest since 1989), highlighting competing views among the committee.

"Recent corporate earnings have reaffirmed our view of the strong corporate fundamentals, underpinned by resilient profit growth."

The updated "dot plot" reflected only one further rate cut in 2026. The committee's quarterly Summary of Economic Projections (SEP) for 2026 showed a significant increase in the GDP forecast from 1.8% to 2.3%, which Federal Reserve Chair Powell partly attributed to business investment relating to datacenters and AI. Labor market conditions continued to be the Federal Reserve's (the "Fed") key motivation to cut rates.

Performance Summary

For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, the Fund's Class I shares returned 1.04%, excluding sales charges. In comparison, the Fund's unmanaged benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, returned 1.10% for the same period.

The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Not FDIC-Insured. Not Bank-Guaranteed. May Lose Value

The US federal government endured the longest shutdown on record at 43 days after Democrats demanded concession around healthcare policy. The government reopened after Congress agreed to pass three of the 12 appropriations bills and a stopgap continuing resolution for the other nine. This left most federal agencies funded through to the end of January 2026. The shutdown delayed several key data releases, such as Consumer Price Index (CPI), where data for October was just uncollected and unpublished.

Data-wise, the labor market continued to loosen. Job growth continued to slow. The unemployment rate rose to 4.6%, the highest since October 2021. However, initial and continuing jobless gains eased slightly. Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation reports were delayed by the shutdown. Reported headline CPI eased from 3% to 2.7% and core CPI eased from 3% to 2.6%, albeit lingering distortions from the shutdown (particularly to the shelter component) was likely a factor. GDP surprised to the upside in the third quarter at 4.3% Seasonally Adjusted Annual Rate (SAAR), stronger than market expectations at 3.3%. Consumption was the bright spot with final private domestic demand rising 3.0%.

On the trade front, the US scaled back a number of agricultural tariffs and announced an agreement with China to reduce tariffs on certain imports while introducing trade framework deals with a number of Southeast Asian and Latin American countries.

It was a strong quarter for global equities, but US stock markets largely underperformed their peers. The US yield curve steepened, with short end yields falling by 14bp and long end yields rising by 11bp. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Corporate Bond Index spreads widened by 4bp, for an excess return of -4bp and a total return of 84bp.

Performance Review

Our duration and yield-curve positioning contributed positively to returns, as did our credit beta. We took profits on our core US duration long and added the rates trades in the “plus” portion of the portfolio. We removed our long rates positions in Canada and added a long in Australian sovereign bonds. We also implemented a relative value short position in Swedish government bonds versus German bunds.

From a credit perspective, sector allocation contributed negatively while security selection had a neutral contribution overall. We increased our spread duration, maintaining overweights across investment grade, high yield, and emerging market corporates. We reduced our exposure to financials and industrials and increased our exposure to utilities.

Market Outlook

We expect US growth to remain positive, but potentially sub-trend in 2026. Manufacturing activity has been under pressure, but we expect AI-related capital expenditure to be supportive.

We believe the labor market may continue to loosen, which should keep the Fed on guard with the aim of preventing it from tipping into a contraction. Tariff inflation may peak in the first half of the year, and the unwinding of CPI survey distortions may result in upward impulses to CPI, even as core services show continued disinflation.

Recent corporate earnings have reaffirmed our view of the strong corporate fundamentals, underpinned by resilient profit growth. We expect capital expenditure borrowing and mergers and acquisitions activity could be a major theme of 2026 in credit markets. However, this supply dynamic appears well-telegraphed to us and is unlikely to surprise markets, albeit if this supply materializes spreads could edge wider and settle into a higher

trading range early in the year. New issuance could nonetheless benefit active managers that can focus on security selection and relative value trades.

We believe investor demand should remain robust in 2026 as investment grade credit can offer a meaningful yield pickup over cash, and in our view, spreads offer attractive returns when compared to fundamental default risks, so we expect demand to remain high.

data.title: Aon Earnings Review: Solid Results Underscore Long-Term Investment Case
data.description:
Today, Aon (AON) released its fourth quarter and full year results. That seems an opposite moment to assess what the results mean for the investment case.

I last covered the name in my 2021 “buy” piece, Aon: Pricier, But Still Attractive, since when it has moved up 36% (solid, but notably less than the S&P 500’s 65% gain over that period).

Q4 and Full-Year Results

At the full year level, it was a solid performance. Revenue was up 9%, with reported organic revenue growth of 6%.

Beneath the topline, performance was also strong. Adjusted EPS was up by 9%, and free cash flow grew 14%.

On balance, that looks like an excellent performance. That is highlighted by some of the toplines the company emphasised.

For a long-established business in a mature industry, that strikes me as positive performance with strong growth.

I think that reflects Aon’s strong brand, deep client relationships, strategic focus, and emphasis on utilising its “3x3” plan and wider “Aon United” strategy discussed below as ways to squeeze more from what it has as well as further enhancing its competitive positioning in the marketplace.

SA had shared an earnings preview ahead of the results. In that, consensus EPS Estimate was $4.75. Actual (adjusted) EPS came in at $4.85, beating the consensus estimate by 2%. That was the third quarter in a row of earnings beating expectations.

The consensus revenue estimate was $4.37bn. Actual revenue of $4.30bn undershot the estimate by 2%.

Balance Sheet Has Improved

The company paid down $1.9bn in net debt over the year. The company said that it expects its balance sheet position to enable it to execute its capital allocation model in the current year, balancing M&A spend with shareholder capital returns.

It expects to spend $1bn on share buybacks this year.

Aon’s 3x3 Programme is Delivering Results

The company pointed to strong execution of its “3x3” strategic plan, part of the so-called “Aon United” strategy.

The charts it presented to support the contention that the savings offered are substantial rely on a belief that, as the programme unfolds, it will become more impactful over time. For now, though, the cash outflow involved of $309m exceeds the cumulative savings of $270m.

The effects (short term and, especially, long term) of such programmes are always hard to quantify with any degree of accuracy, in my view.

But the message here is that the programme is delivering benefits over time, which presumably should ultimately mean the savings will exceed the cost. The company said that,

“Our strong execution of the 3x3 Plan is deepening client impact, sharpening our competitive differentiation, and reinforcing the durability of our model – which underpins our ability to drive recurring new wins, expand existing relationships, sustain high retention, and accelerate operating leverage through (Aon Business Services).”

To some extent there is always a fair degree of judgment required when assessing the actual impact of such programmes versus the hypothetical of what might have happened in their absence. But I do buy into the idea that this integrated programme of risk capital and human capital capabilities, powered by Aon Business Services, and innovative capital solutions is helping to deliver new business wins as well as strengthen existing relationships, which over time, if executed properly, ought to improve business performance.

Aon sounds confident that it is already doing that. It highlighted the fact that it is helping the firm better differentiate itself versus competitors as well as reinforcing the durability of its model. As geopolitical volatility and business uncertainty exacerbate a general lack of clarity in the business community about what is coming down the road, that seems like a compelling message to be presenting to clients and potential clients.

Valuation Looks Reasonable for the Growth Prospects

The company shared an outlook of “clear visibility to delivering mid-single-digit or greater organic revenue growth, 70-80 bps of adjusted operating margin expansion, strong adjusted EPS growth and double-digit free cash flow growth.” Based on its performance over the past several years, I believe it is reasonable to expect the firm to deliver on that.

Last time I wrote Aon it was with a “buy” rating and I maintain that rating.

Risks include price competition from rivals, something I believe the 3x3 and Aon United approaches could help mitigate by moving the conversation about value proposition further beyond price alone.

The share trades on a P/E ratio of 20. That does not strike me as cheap exactly, but in the current market I think it is reasonable for a company of Aon’s quality with the ongoing growth potential it has, as demonstrated in the latest set of results.

At 0.9%, the yield is not especially attractive, and even with steady dividend increases, I do not see Aon becoming a compelling income story any time soon.

But the steady, long-term growth opportunity here is appealing in my view.

data.title: Invesco Emerging Markets Ex-China Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 New portfolio positioning in place

As announced on June 23, 2025, the fund transitioned to new leadership under the Asian and Emerging Markets Equities Investment Team, which resulted in a change in its investment philosophy and process.

2 The fund underperformed its benchmark

The fund had a positive return for the quarter but underperformed the MSCI EM ex China Index. Stock selection and an overweight in consumer discretionary was the largest detractor from relative return.

3 Emerging markets ex China opportunity

We believe emerging market equities (excluding China) currently offer strong earnings growth potential, with reasonable valuation levels across much of the universe.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Emerging market equities, excluding China, delivered a positive return for the quarter, outpacing developed international equities, US equities and the broader emerging market universe.
  • Continued strong performance from artificial intelligence-related (AI) stocks in Taiwan and from technology stocks in South Korea helped to lift the MSCI EM ex China Index during the quarter. The US dollar's continued weakness also boosted emerging market equities.
  • We believe emerging market ex-China equities currently offer strong earnings growth potential, with reasonable valuation levels across much of the universe. Nevertheless, the asset class has still been trading at what we see as a significant discount to global equities, particularly the US market.
  • Though we remain mindful of geopolitical risks and uncertainty that may come with the US government's pursuit of protectionist policies, emerging market corporations generally in our view have healthy balance sheets and competitive advantages, which could make them more resilient than what is currently implied in valuations.
  • Moreover, if specific global trade channels are forced to reconfigure away from China, other emerging market countries could benefit, which would likely foster further growth in trade among emerging countries.

data.title: Deckers: Market Share Gains Are Propelling A Low Valuation (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
As we move through a volatile Q4 earnings season, one trend is crystallizing in the market: a rotation is underway. Investors are beginning to shun the large-cap tech stocks like Microsoft (MSFT) that drove much of last year's gains, while pouring more money into fallen value-oriented stocks.

Deckers (DECK) is a great example of this. The parent company behind HOKA running shoes and UGG boots just posted fiscal Q3 (calendar Q4) results and produced one of the biggest one-day gains in the stock in recent memory. Deckers shot up ~20% post-earnings, though it remains down about ~50% over the past year: the stock still has a long ways to go to reclaim prior heights.

I last wrote an article upgrading Deckers to a neutral rating in October, when the stock was trading just shy of $90 per share. Since then, Deckers has shot up more than 30% higher, though that jump has also been accompanied by very healthy HOKA results, as well as an acceleration in DTC sales. On top of this, Deckers is retaining healthy gross margins, despite the onset of tariffs. I'm upgrading the stock to a buy.

For investors who are newer to Deckers, here's a rundown of my top reasons to buy the stock:

  • Market share gains for HOKA. HOKA running shoes are gaining ground right as Nike is losing its dominance in footwear. HOKA's mid-teens sales growth is positioning the company to join the ranks of ascendant running shoe brands (alongside On (ONON), the Swiss giant that is perhaps the fastest-growing label) that is taking on Nike.
  • Shoes have definite replacement cycles. Serious runners, which is the market that Deckers caters to with HOKA, typically replace their running shoes after every 300-500 miles (which is a threshold quickly attained for serious distance runners). This makes the repurchase of shoes a necessity rather than a luxury, which can drive demand even in a weakened consumer spending environment.
  • UGG provides strong diversification. Deckers' ownership of UGG, which also continues to grow in the mid-single digits, also provides solid diversification to a different set of customers as well as timeless style.
  • Elevated gross margins. Deckers boasts gross margins in the ~60% range, which skews on the high end for a consumer products maker. The company has also barely lost ground on gross margins, despite the fact that tariffs continue to be a major headwind.

The biggest reason to buy Deckers, however, is a reasonable valuation - especially against a raised outlook. For the remainder of FY26 (which for Deckers ends in March), the company has boosted its full-year sales expectations to $5.40-$5.425 billion, or 8-9% growth. It's now expecting mid-teens sales growth for HOKA and mid-single-digit growth for UGG, versus low-teens for HOKA and low- to mid-single-digit for UGG expected previously. This stacks well on top of a 100 bps boost to gross margin expectations, yielding full-year EPS of $6.80-$6.85, versus $6.30-$6.39 previously.

For next year FY27, Wall Street is expecting Deckers to hit $5.80 billion in revenue (7% sales growth) and $7.12 in pro forma EPS. This positions Deckers' valuation at:

  • 17.4x FY26 P/E
  • 16.7x FY27 P/E

Since balance sheet cash represents roughly 11% of Deckers' current market value, we note that ex-cash P/E multiples are also about 11% lower than as-reported. We note that many names in the apparel sector, especially direct shoe rival Nike (NKE), trade at substantial premiums despite the fact that HOKA is gaining ground:

In my view, with many fundamental clouds lifting for Deckers, it's a good time to buy this stock on recent strength.

Q3 recap

Let's now run through Deckers' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Deckers' revenue grew 7% y/y to $1.96 billion in Q3, substantially outpacing Wall Street's much more conservative expectations of $1.87 billion (+2% y/y) by a wide five-point margin. It's clear that expectations were low for the holiday season after reports of tepid consumer spending: but Deckers managed to buck the trend.

The snapshot below, meanwhile, showcases core details by brand and by channel. HOKA surged with 18.5% y/y growth to $629 million in revenue, or roughly a third of sales, while UGG also achieved 4.9% y/y growth to $1.30 billion in revenue.

One of the earlier concerns with Deckers was that wholesale revenue had been outpacing DTC revenue. In the prior quarter, wholesale had grown at a 13% y/y pace, while DTC fell -1% y/y. My concern here was that Deckers' growth in previous quarters may have been driven by "channel stuffing" - or selling inventory to retailers that may have a harder time selling through the product, which would indicate lower restocking shipments.

The good news in this quarter (and one of the reasons why I'm upgrading the stock to a buy) is that DTC recovered to 8% y/y growth, which is a very healthy indicator of end-customer demand in a shakier macro climate. Deckers does not report sell-through, so we take the strong DTC demand to indicate that brand momentum is overall in good shape, and that the wholesale channel is indeed moving through inventory at a normal pace. The company notes that its wholesale inventory positioning, in fact, is what helped to drive strong sales, particularly for UGG, where improved in-stocks helped to boost visibility and drive meaningful international growth. Per CEO Stefano Caroti's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

As discussed on our last call, in response to the ongoing rise in consumer demand for the UGG brand, we strategically allocated additional products to the wholesale channel prior to peak season. The results indicate that this approach has proven effective.

Our strategic execution enabled improved in-stock positions for our wholesale partners, boosting fall sales and as planned, we effectively address late season demand through our direct-to-consumer channels. In terms of the UGG brand's third quarter performance across channels, DTC revenue increased 5% versus last year and wholesale revenue grew 4% compared to last year. From a direct-to-consumer perspective, our marketplace teams around the globe work closely across different departments to fill consumer demand, both in retail locations and online. Through these efforts, we drove meaningful growth in UGG Rewards membership, e-mail subscribers and retain consumers, providing ample opportunity to further strengthen consumer connections and drive repeat purchases in the future."

Profitability remained quite healthy as well. Gross margins slipped only -50 bps y/y to a still-high 59.8%, despite the impact of tariffs. The company also delivered $3.33 in pro forma EPS, up 11% y/y and beating Street expectations of $2.77 with a meaningful 20% upside.

Key takeaways

With very strong December quarter results in a time that many investors were concerned about holiday spending in the wake of a weaker consumer environment, Deckers is putting itself back on the map, especially with HOKA growing double digits and taking share against Nike. With Deckers still trading at a mid-teens P/E ratio, the stock still has room to climb higher as long as it continues to execute. I continue to be encouraged by signals of a market rotation away from tech and into unloved sectors like retail: buy here on recent strength.

data.title: Ace River Capital Q4 2025 Quarterly Letter
data.description:
Dear Investors,

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Ace River Capital Partners, L.P. (the "Fund") returned -4.55%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 (SPX) and Russell 2000 (RTY) returned +2.7% and +2.2%, respectively. For the full year, the Fund returned -21.95%, compared to +18.0% for the S&P 500 and +12.8% for the Russell 2000.

While these results were clearly disappointing, the year was defined far more by valuation compression and idiosyncratic events than by any permanent impairment of underlying business value. As always, my focus remains on long-term intrinsic value rather than short-term price movements.

Cadence Note: Beginning in 2026, investor communications will shift from a quarterly schedule to semiannual updates. This change reflects the Fund's long-term, low-turnover approach and emphasis on intrinsic value over short-term volatility. Interim updates will be provided as warranted by material developments.

Strategy

Ace River Capital maintains a highly concentrated portfolio of small- and micro-cap businesses with durable economics, tangible assets, and long runways for reinvestment. I focus on companies where competitive advantages are structural rather than ephemeral, and where capital can be redeployed at attractive rates over long periods of time.

The portfolio intentionally avoids software, finance, and insurance businesses. Instead, it emphasizes asset-backed businesses with scarcity value, including:

  • special-purpose licensed real estate (RCI Hospitality),
  • waterfront and marina assets (MarineMax),
  • and royalty interests in mineral deposits (Vox Royalty).

This approach requires patience, deep research, and the willingness to tolerate volatility. I typically hold fewer than five positions, follow them closely, and only add when the risk-reward profile is clearly asymmetric. There will be extended periods of inactivity, limited trading, and occasional sharp drawdowns. This is a feature of the strategy, not a flaw.

Portfolio Update

Vox Royalty Corp. (VOXR):

Since initiating a position in Vox Royalty in Q4 2023, it has grown into the Fund's largest holding and has returned approximately 160% at the time of writing. Gold prices continue to reach record highs, driven by inflation concerns, geopolitical instability, and inconsistent trade and fiscal policy.

Vox's royalty-based business model provides leveraged exposure to these trends while avoiding the capital intensity and operational risks faced by traditional mining companies.

The company continues to add producing royalties, expand cash flow, and maintain a clean balance sheet. I view Vox as both a long-term compounder and a natural hedge against macro uncertainty. No change in thesis.

RCI Hospitality Holdings (RICK):

During the fourth quarter, RCI Hospitality shares declined roughly 25% following the filing of bribery and tax-related charges against the CEO, CFO, and other executives by the New York Attorney General in September 2025. These developments created significant legal and governance uncertainty and drove the stock to what I would describe as crisis valuation without crisis operations.

Importantly, the core business remains intact. Cash flow continues, share repurchases have not stopped, and the company now trades below the estimated value of its owned real estate, effectively assigning little value to ongoing earnings power.

Short interest has increased to roughly 12% of the float, further compressing an already small share base following recent buybacks. The CEO and CFO have since stepped down, reducing governance risk, and while there may be fines or balance-sheet impacts ahead, I believe much of this risk is already reflected in the stock price.

The long-term thesis is unchanged. I view current prices as an opportunity and expect to add to the position as conditions warrant.

MarineMax (HZO):

MarineMax remains an attractive long-term asset owner with a valuable portfolio of marinas and waterfront real estate. However, absent near-term catalysts and given the cyclical nature of the retail boating business, I elected to sell in-the-money covered calls during the quarter. The shares were called away, and the position was reduced by roughly half.

While I like management's stated shift toward higher-margin service, storage, and marina revenue, it remains unclear how much of this mix shift reflects structural improvement versus cyclical weakness in retail sales. I continue to monitor the position but will remain selective in deploying additional capital.

DSHK / GLFE Update (Speculative Position):

During the period, Drive Shack Inc. (DSHK) completed a corporate restructuring that resulted in the rebranding of the equity to Green Light Financial Enterprises (GLFE) along with a change in listing status and trading venue. This transition reflects the company's ongoing efforts to reposition its assets and capital structure following the wind‑down of its legacy entertainment operations.

The position remains highly speculative and illiquid and is sized accordingly. While near‑term fundamentals remain challenged, the thesis is driven by optionality around asset monetization, balance‑sheet outcomes, and any improvement in liquidity or strategic direction under the new structure. We do not intend to add to the position and will look to reduce exposure opportunistically if liquidity improves.

Looking Ahead

Markets continue to reward a narrow group of large-cap technology companies, while small-caps remain broadly ignored. History suggests these periods of dislocation tend to create some of the best long-term opportunities.

I remain patient, focused, and prepared to act when valuations become compelling. Volatility does not concern me; permanent capital impairment does.

Fund Positions - In Order of Portfolio Weight

VOXR, RICK, HZO, DSHK

Thank you for reading. Please reach out for any reason.

Nicholas D'Agnillo | Ace River Capital Partners | Nick@AceRiverCapital.com | 401-499-3935
